functional_status.assistance_hygiene_some: I am generally independent with most hygiene tasks such as brushing my teeth, washing my face, and toileting, but I do need some assistance with a few activities because of lower back pain and occasional weakness in my right leg. For example, my wife usually helps me when I need to get in and out of the bathtub, as stepping over the tub edge and lowering myself down can aggravate my back and sometimes causes my leg to give way. I also occasionally need help with drying my lower legs and feet after a shower, since bending forward for more than a few seconds increases my pain significantly. On bad days, she helps me with putting on socks and tying my shoes, especially on my right side, because the combination of bending and twisting is difficult for me. I am still able to manage upper-body dressing and grooming on my own, but anything that involves prolonged bending at the waist or balancing on one leg, like trimming toenails, is challenging and I either avoid it or ask for help to prevent flare-ups.

medical_and_dietary_information.medication_list:
1. Lisinopril 10 mg tablet – once daily in the morning for high blood pressure (taking for approximately 4 years).
2. Atorvastatin 20 mg tablet – once daily at night for high cholesterol (taking for approximately 3 years).
3. Ibuprofen 400 mg tablet – taken as needed, usually up to 2–3 times per week in the late afternoon or evening when lower back pain increases, always with food to avoid stomach upset.
4. Acetaminophen 500 mg tablet – taken occasionally (no more than 2,000 mg per day) on days when I try to avoid ibuprofen or when pain is milder but still distracting.
5. Vitamin D3 2,000 IU – once daily with breakfast, recommended by my primary care doctor after mildly low vitamin D levels were found.
6. Over-the-counter magnesium supplement (250 mg) – once daily in the evening to help with mild muscle tension and nighttime leg cramps.

I am not currently taking any blood thinners, steroids, or strong prescription pain medications. I do not have any known medication allergies, but I do tend to get mild heartburn if I take ibuprofen on an empty stomach, so I always take it with food and water.
medical_and_dietary_information.diet_list:
I try to follow a relatively balanced Mediterranean-style diet recommended by my primary care physician to support heart health and weight control. On a typical weekday, I eat oatmeal with berries and a small amount of nuts for breakfast, along with black coffee and water. For lunch, I usually have a mixed salad (lettuce, tomatoes, cucumbers, bell peppers) with grilled chicken or canned tuna, dressed with olive oil and lemon juice, plus a slice or two of whole-grain bread. Dinner is often baked or grilled fish or chicken, brown rice or whole-wheat pasta, and steamed or roasted vegetables such as broccoli, carrots, or green beans. I try to limit red meat to once a week (often on weekends, like a small steak or lean ground beef in a homemade sauce) and include plant-based meals (such as lentil soup, chickpea curry, or bean chili) at least twice a week.

I make an effort to reduce processed foods and added sugars, so I rarely drink regular soda and instead choose water or unsweetened herbal tea. I have cut down significantly on desserts; I might have a small piece of dark chocolate or fruit after dinner instead of pastries or ice cream. I also try to avoid very salty snacks like chips because of my blood pressure, although I do occasionally have them on social occasions. I limit alcohol to 1–2 glasses of red wine per week, usually with dinner on the weekend.

While I do not strictly count calories, I am mindful of portion sizes because I would like to lose about 5–7 kilograms to ease pressure on my lower back. I generally eat three main meals and a small afternoon snack (like a yogurt or a piece of fruit) to keep my energy stable throughout the day. I have no food allergies that I know of, but I have noticed that very heavy, greasy meals (fried foods, fast food, or large late-night meals) tend to make my back and leg feel more inflamed the next morning and also disturb my sleep, so I try to avoid those as much as possible.
